title: Baked vanilla cheesecake
subtitle: New York style: dense, smooth, cracked-free
excerpt: A classic baked cream-cheese cheesecake on a butter biscuit base. Baking in a water bath is the step that keeps the surface smooth and prevents the top from cracking.
sourceNotes: William Lawrence developed cream cheese commercially in New York in 1872; the ingredient was quickly adopted into American baking. Mrs Rorer, Mrs Rorer's New Cook Book (1902): cream cheese pastry and dessert recipes in early American use. Irma Rombauer, Joy of Cooking (1936 and later editions): cheesecake recipes documented across 20th-century American household baking. The water-bath baking technique for custard-style bakes is standard in professional pastry and documented in Escoffier, Le Guide Culinaire (1903), translated edition.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the fully cooled cheesecake, uncovered until solid, then wrap in cling film for up to 2 months. Defrost in the fridge overnight. The texture softens very slightly but remains good.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The cheesecake is best made a day ahead. The flavour and texture both improve after an overnight chill.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; low temperature is what keeps the custard-like filling from curdling or cracking

recipeTools.notes: 20 cm, with a tight seal on the base.
To wrap the outside of the springform tin before the water bath.
Large enough to hold the springform tin with room for water.

glossaryTerms.definition: A container of hot water in which a second vessel sits during cooking or baking. The water regulates temperature, preventing the contents from exceeding 100°C and buffering against sudden heat changes. In baked cheesecake, a water bath keeps the oven atmosphere humid and the sides of the tin from baking faster than the centre, which is the primary cause of surface cracking.

content.text: A baked cheesecake is a set custard with a biscuit base. The filling is cream cheese, eggs, soured cream, sugar, and vanilla, whisked together and baked low and slow until the proteins in the eggs set the mixture to a dense, sliceable texture. It has very little in common with a no-bake cheesecake set with gelatine; the baked version is richer, denser, and significantly more satisfying.

The detail that separates a smooth, uncracked cheesecake from a split, dry one is the baking temperature. Too hot and the filling sets unevenly, with the outside overdone before the centre is cooked. Baking in a water bath wraps the tin in a layer of moisture that moderates the temperature around the cheesecake and keeps the surface from drying out before the interior is ready.
What you need

Use full-fat cream cheese. Reduced-fat versions contain more water, which produces a filling that sets looser and is prone to weeping after slicing. All filling ingredients should be at room temperature before mixing; cold cream cheese mixed with room-temperature eggs produces an uneven batter with lumps that do not smooth out in baking.
Method
Make the biscuit base

Heat the oven to 155°C fan. Grease the base and sides of the 20 cm springform tin and line the base with a disc of baking paper. Crush {{digestive-biscuit}} the digestive biscuits to fine crumbs — a food processor takes 30 seconds, or seal them in a bag and crush with a rolling pin. Mix the crumbs with {{unsalted-butter}} melted butter until every crumb is coated. Press firmly into the base of the tin in an even layer. Bake for 10 minutes, then leave to cool while you make the filling. The baked base will be firmer and less prone to crumbling when the cheesecake is sliced.
Make the filling

Beat {{cream-cheese}} cream cheese in a large bowl until smooth, then add {{caster-sugar}} caster sugar and beat again until combined. Add {{soured-cream}} soured cream, {{vanilla-extract}} vanilla extract, and the lemon zest and mix through. Add {{eggs}} the eggs one at a time, mixing well after each. The finished batter should be smooth, glossy, and completely lump-free. Do not over-mix once the eggs are in; excessive mixing incorporates air, which causes the surface to crack during baking.
Prepare the water bath

Wrap the outside of the springform tin, covering both base and sides, in a double layer of cling film pressed tightly to form a watertight seal. Place the wrapped tin inside the large rectangular baking tin. This is the
bain-marie
setup. Pour the cheesecake batter over the cooled biscuit base, then pour boiling water into the outer tin until it reaches about 2 cm up the sides of the springform tin.
Bake and cool

Bake at 155°C fan for 50–55 minutes. The cheesecake is done when the edges are set and opaque, the surface no longer looks wet, and the centre has a very slight wobble when the tin is gently shaken — roughly the size of a 50p coin, no more. The wobble will firm completely as it cools.

Turn the oven off. Open the oven door a few centimetres and leave the cheesecake inside for 30 minutes. This gradual cooling prevents the dramatic temperature change that causes the filling to contract and crack. After 30 minutes, transfer to a cooling rack and cool to room temperature, then refrigerate for at least 4 hours or overnight before slicing.
Serve

Run a thin knife around the inside edge of the tin before releasing the springform clip. Slice with a knife dipped in hot water and wiped clean between cuts. Serve at room temperature, not straight from the fridge; the texture is noticeably better when the cheesecake has had 20 minutes to warm slightly.
Troubleshooting

items.symptom: Surface cracked across the top during baking
items.cause: Oven too hot, cheesecake overbaked, water bath not used, or filling over-mixed after the eggs went in.
items.fix: Use an oven thermometer to verify temperature. The filling should still have a slight wobble in the centre when you remove it. Crack or no crack, a cracked cheesecake still tastes the same; cover the top with a spoonful of soured cream before serving.

items.symptom: Filling is grainy or slightly curdled after baking
items.cause: Cream cheese or eggs were too cold when mixed, producing an uneven emulsion.
items.fix: Leave cream cheese and eggs at room temperature for at least 30 minutes before mixing. For future batches, beat the cream cheese until completely smooth before adding any other ingredient.

items.symptom: Water got into the tin through the cling film and made the base soggy
items.cause: Single layer of cling film with a gap at the top of the tin, or a loose seal.
items.fix: Use a double layer of cling film pressed tightly up the sides of the tin and over the rim. Some bakers prefer two layers of heavy-duty kitchen foil as an alternative. Check the seal before adding the water.

content.text: A note on the style

The baked cream-cheese cheesecake became an American standard in the late 19th century after William Lawrence developed commercially available cream cheese in 1872. The dense, very rich New York style associated with restaurants like Lindy's in the 1920s became the benchmark for the genre. This recipe is a domestic version of that style: less rich than a restaurant version, but with the same emphasis on density and a clean vanilla flavour rather than a topped-with-fruit finish.
